note: I'm implementing deep-merge behavior in Features.update(). Only touching features.py.

type: PROPOSE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: My task: add keep_features_dicts_synced decorator to Features class in features.py to sync _column_requires_decoding dict on __setitem__/__delitem__/update etc. Need to own features.py. What is your feature/files?

type: REVISE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: My task: make Features.update() deep-merge nested dict values instead of overwriting. Both our features touch features.py (mine: body of update() method; yours: decorator wrapping __setitem__/__delitem__/update/etc). Since we can't get disjoint files, propose sequencing: I'll implement my update() body change first (small, isolated edit) and send DONE. You then pull latest features.py and wrap your keep_features_dicts_synced decorator around update/__setitem__/__delitem__ on top of my change. That way your decorator wraps my merge logic without us clobbering each other. OK?

type: REVISE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Same file, unavoidable overlap - let's sequence instead of split. Plan: (1) I add a keep_features_dicts_synced decorator + apply it to __setitem__, __delitem__, update, pop, popitem, clear, setdefault on the Features class (only touching decorator lines/signatures + adding thin overrides for pop/popitem/clear/setdefault, NOT the internal merge logic of update's body). (2) I'll send DONE when finished. (3) You then implement deep-merge logic strictly inside update()'s body (the loop/merge logic), keeping the @keep_features_dicts_synced decorator line I added intact above it. This way we touch disjoint regions: I own decorator infra + method signatures/wrapping, you own update()'s internal merge algorithm. Reply ACCEPT if ok, or REVISE with alternative.

type: ACCEPT

note: Agreed. Waiting for your DONE signal before I edit update()'s internal merge logic.
